outcome.headline: Sorted G:H (NAME, REF) is exactly correct; agent copied original column A values into output column F instead of renumbering the deduplicated/reordered list sequentially 1..N, failing the cell-by-cell verifier on every F row.

failure_modes.name: Misinterpreted ITEM column F as carry-over of original A rather than re-numbered sequence

failure_modes.name: Superficial output validation

failure_modes.description: Steps 7 and 14 re-read the produced output and inspected F:H rows, but the agent only compared the result to its own intent rather than reasoning about whether ITEM should be re-sequenced; it iterated 5 more times tweaking unrelated edge cases (case-insensitive dedup, header detection, empty-A handling) without revisiting the F-numbering choice.
failure_modes.evidence_quote:
for i in range(1, 15):

failure_modes.name: Manual verification missed the visible F-column mismatch

failure_modes.name: Skipped benchmark verifier
failure_modes.description: After writing the output workbook, the agent only re-read F:H with ad-hoc openpyxl prints and never executed /tests/evaluate.py or test.sh, so the reward-0 cell mismatch from harbor was never surfaced or fixed during the run.

counterfactual.Y: Manually printed rows from /app/output/1_22-47_output.xlsx and treated the task as complete without the official verifier.

counterfactual.Y: Manually printed rows from /app/output/1_22-47_output.xlsx and treated the task as complete without the official verifier.

failure_modes.name: Sort-rule iteration churn
failure_modes.description: From steps 9-13 the agent rewrote the full sorter repeatedly, oscillating on whether to require non-empty A/C, case-sensitive duplicates, answer_position bounds, and clearing ranges—adding latency without converging on a validated implementation before stopping.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: I've also relaxed my prior criteria to account for valid entries in B and C even if A is empty

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Verifier does deterministic cell-by-cell comparison against a golden answer xlsx in F2:H20; passing requires matching ground-truth values, not just plausible sort output on the visible input.

outcome.headline: The agent sorted rows correctly by helper column J but left original ITEM numbers in column F instead of renumbering them 1..n as the golden workbook requires.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: tests/evaluate.py compares each cell in F2:H20 of /app/output/1_22-47_output.xlsx to 1_22-47_answer.xlsx (cell_level_compare, lines 108-121).
outcome.what_agent_produced: An openpyxl script (last executed as solution_final2.py in step 13) that deduplicated on (B,C), ordered rows by helper J names HASSAN/HAMAN/HAMMED then remaining source order, and wrote sorted rows to F2:H11 while copying original column-A ITEM values into F (e.g., F2=5, F3=1).

failure_modes.name: Dropped ITEM renumbering
failure_modes.description: In step 5 the agent concluded column F must be rewritten as a sequential ITEM series, but every executed solution through step 13 still assigned ws.cell(..., column=6).value = d['a'], so all ten output ITEM cells disagreed with the answer file (expected 1..10).
failure_modes.evidence_quote: I've confirmed that the ITEM (A) column has to be rewritten so that it's sequential.

counterfactual.X: When writing sorted rows to F2:H, set column F to enumerate(1, len(sorted_data)+1) instead of d['a'].
counterfactual.Y: Copied each row's original column-A value into column F with ws.cell(row=out_r, column=6).value = d['a'].

failure_modes.name: Superficial output validation
failure_modes.description: After writing output, the agent inspected F:H with openpyxl (steps 7 and 14), saw the expected NAME/REF ordering, and treated the solution as correct without checking that column F matched the sequential ITEM numbering in the golden answer.

issues: instruction.md does not explicitly state whether column F should copy source ITEM values or be renumbered 1..n; the golden answer requires sequential renumbering while preserving original B/C pairs.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Verifier performs direct openpyxl value equality on F2:H20 against a private answer xlsx; passing requires correct cell values, not a shortcut check.

outcome.headline: The agent sorted and deduplicated rows into F2:H in roughly the right J-driven order but kept original ITEM values in column F instead of renumbering them, so the verifier’s F2:H20 cell comparison scored 0.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: Cell-by-cell equality of agent output vs golden answer for range F2:H20 on sheet1 (`evaluate.py` `compare_workbooks` → `cell_level_compare`, `config.json` answer_position).
outcome.what_agent_produced: Python/openpyxl pipeline that reads helper column J, deduplicates rows on (B,C), sorts J-listed names first then remaining rows in source order, and writes A:C into F2:H while preserving source ITEM integers (e.g. F2=5 with HASSAN, F3=1 with HASSAN).

failure_modes.name: ITEM column not renumbered
failure_modes.description: During planning the agent concluded column F should use sequential ITEM numbers, but every save path (from the first `/tmp/solution.py` through the final script) wrote `d['a']` unchanged. Step 7 self-check showed F2:H with values like `[5,'HASSAN',133444422]` and `[1,'HASSAN',123444441]`, which diverges from a typical SpreadsheetBench answer that reindexes ITEM 1..n in the output block.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: I've confirmed that the ITEM (A) column has to be rewritten so that it's sequential.

counterfactual.X: When writing sorted rows to F2:H, set column F to 1,2,3,... (sequential ITEM) instead of `d['a']`.
counterfactual.Y: Assigned `ws.cell(row=out_r, column=6).value = d['a']`, preserving source ITEM numbers like 5 and 1.

failure_modes.name: No official verifier run
failure_modes.description: After producing `/app/output/1_22-47_output.xlsx`, the agent only pretty-printed F:H with ad-hoc openpyxl reads (steps 7 and 14) and never invoked `/tests/evaluate.py` or compared against the answer workbook before finishing.

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Verifier compares agent output xlsx to a hidden golden file over the declared F2:H20 range with per-cell value equality; passing requires matching the reference sheet, not shortcut checks.

outcome.headline: The agent sorted NAME/REF correctly by helper column J but copied original ITEM values into column F instead of renumbering 1–n as required.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: Cell-by-cell equality in F2:H20 between /app/output/1_22-47_output.xlsx and the answer workbook (evaluate.py compare_workbooks / cell_level_compare).
outcome.what_agent_produced: An openpyxl script wrote ten deduplicated, J-ordered rows into F2:H11 with correct NAME and REF but preserved source column-A ITEM numbers (e.g., F2=5) rather than sequential 1–10.

failure_modes.name: ITEM column not renumbered
failure_modes.description: The agent stated the ITEM column must be rewritten sequentially, then every solution wrote d['a'] into column F. Ground-truth F2:H11 uses ITEM 1–10 while NAME/REF match the agent’s sort order.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: the ITEM (A) column has to be rewritten so that it's sequential.

counterfactual.X: When writing sorted rows to F2:H, set column F to enumerate(out_r - 1, start=1) instead of d['a'].
counterfactual.Y: Set ws.cell(row=out_r, column=6).value = d['a'] in every final script.

failure_modes.name: ITEM validation omitted
failure_modes.description: After generating output, the agent inspected F:H for name order only and concluded the solution was correct without checking that column F matched sequential ITEM numbering.

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Verifier compares output cells to a hidden answer file; passing requires correct F:H values, not test-script tricks.

aggregated_from.note: Failure modes: union of all judge×round audits, deduped by (A,B,C,D). Outcome/verdicts: majority vote (step_where_lost = median). Individual passes are kept under `passes`. Step indices remapped from the audits' 1-indexing to the viewer's 0-indexing.
